nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving coastal lagoons, swamps, canals, and tidal flats along the Yucatan Peninsula; highly vegetated shallow margins with access to brackish and marine conditions

decorPreferences: dense planting (hornwort, water sprite, vallisneria, floating plants)
open swimming lanes for male dorsal display
algae-covered surfaces and rocks for grazing
driftwood or smooth rocks as territory markers

diet.notes: Strongly herbivore-leaning. Staple diet should emphasise algae wafers, spirulina flakes, blanched vegetables (zucchini, spinach, cucumber), and vegetable-based pellets — wild fish feed primarily on algae and epiphytic matter. Also accepts high-quality flake, small pellets, and frozen or live foods (brine shrimp, daphnia, bloodworm) as occasional protein supplement. Constant access to algae growth or supplemental greens reduces soft-plant nibbling. Feeding 2–3 small portions daily is preferred.

avoidWith: cherry shrimp and small dwarf shrimp (fry predation risk)
slow-moving or long-finned fish (fin nibbling risk)
small fish that may be harassed by dominant males

summary: The largest of the “true” sailfin mollies, males develop a magnificent sail-like dorsal fin spanning the full body height. Endemic to Mexico’s Yucatan Peninsula. Euryhaline and brackish-tolerant; thrives in hard, alkaline water. Numerous captive colour forms include Lyretail, Marble, and Silver variants.
careNotes:
Poecilia velifera, the Yucatan Molly, is widely regarded as the largest of the “true” sailfin mollies, with males capable of reaching 15–18 cm total length and developing a spectacular dorsal fin that can equal or exceed their body depth when fully erect. It is closely related to the Sailfin Molly (P. latipinna, fw-fish-092), which overlaps in appearance and is frequently confused with it in the trade; P. velifera is distinguished by a higher dorsal fin ray count (18–19 rays vs 14 in P. latipinna) and larger adult size. The species is endemic to the coastal lagoons and tidal flats of the Yucatan Peninsula, Mexico.

A tank of at least 200 L with a minimum 150 cm footprint is strongly recommended to give males room to display and to accommodate a healthy social group (one male to two or more females). Maintain hard, alkaline water: gH 15–30, kH 8–20, pH 7.5–8.5; soft or acidic water is associated with increased disease susceptibility, fin deterioration, and shortened lifespan. Mineral supplementation is advisable in soft-water areas. A 2:1 or 3:1 female-to-male ratio is generally recommended to distribute male harassment.

P. velifera is euryhaline — it naturally inhabits brackish and occasionally marine-adjacent waters — and many experienced keepers report improved vitality, reduced fungal and bacterial infections, and better fin condition when low-level marine salt is added (SG 1.002–1.005). This is beneficial but not strictly required. The diet should be predominantly plant-based; algae wafers, spirulina products, and blanched vegetables form the staple, supplemented by occasional frozen protein foods.

Numerous selectively-bred colour and fin forms are widely available in the hobby, including Lyretail (lyre-shaped caudal and extended dorsal), Marble (mottled black-and-white), and Silver (platinum) morphs. Captive-bred strains are often hardier than wild-type fish and adapt more readily to standard tap water. Many fish sold simply as “Giant Sailfin Molly” or “Yucatan Molly” in the trade may be P. velifera–P. latipinna hybrids; pure P. velifera males are best identified by the high dorsal ray count.
breedingNotes: Prolific livebearer with gestation of approximately 6–8 weeks; broods typically range from 20 to 80 or more fry depending on female size and condition. Females store sperm and can produce multiple successive broods without re-mating. Fry are born large relative to most livebearers and accept finely crushed flake, spirulina powder, and micro-pellets from birth. No parental care is provided; dense planting (floating plants and fine-leaved species) substantially improves fry survival. A 2–3:1 female-to-male ratio reduces male harassment and stress on individual females. Males begin developing the characteristic dorsal sail extension as they reach sexual maturity, typically around 3–4 months of age.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Poecilia velifera
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (males to 18 cm TL), temperature range, pH, hardness, native range (Yucatan Peninsula, Mexico), habitat, diet, breeding, and euryhaline ecology. Distinguishes P. velifera from P. latipinna by higher dorsal ray count (18–19 vs 14).

additional.name: FishBase — Poecilia velifera
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (males to 18 cm TL), temperature tolerance (22–28°C), native range restricted to Yucatan Peninsula, taxonomy (Poeciliidae, Cyprinodontiformes), and habitat type. Confirmed euryhaline designation.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Molly Fish Care
additional.notes: General molly care guide covering all common Poecilia species. Tank size 29–55 gal (110–208 L) for larger mollies; 200 L chosen for P. velifera given its larger adult size vs P. latipinna. Confirmed temperature 24–27°C, salt tolerance, hard alkaline preference, and herbivore-leaning omnivore diet.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Sailfin Molly species guide
additional.notes: Supplementary source. Confirmed P. velifera as the largest sailfin molly species, endemic to Yucatan coastal waters, with dorsal fin reaching full body height in dominant males. Notes on selectively-bred forms (Lyretail, Marble, Silver) and trade identification challenges between P. velifera and P. latipinna hybrids.

fish.breedingNotes: Prolific livebearer; gestation ~6–8 weeks, 20–80+ fry per brood. Female sperm storage enables successive broods. No specialised setup required beyond 2–3:1 female-to-male ratio and dense planting for fry refuge. Fry accept micro-crushed flake and spirulina powder from birth.
